And now permit me to turn to Paragraph 5 of this self-same decree of Hitler's which states that "in cases of divergencies of opinion between the Governor General and the Reichsfuehrer of the SS and the German Police, my decision is to be obtained through the Reich Minister and the Head of the Reich Chancellery." In this connection I would ask you, does not this paragraph testify to the very considerable rights granted by you to the leaders of the police and the SS in the Government General and to your own responsibility for the activities of these organizations?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: The wording of the decree testifies to it, but the actual development was quite the contrary. I believe that we will come to that in detail. I maintain therefore that this attempt to gain some influence over the police and the SS also failed.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Then may I ask whose attempt it was? In this case it is evidently an attempt by Hitler for he signed this decree. Krueger was evidently more powerful than Hitler?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That question is not quite clear to me. You mean that Krueger went against the decree of the Fuehrer?, Of course he did, but that has nothing to do with power. That was considered by Himmler as a tremendous concession made to me. I want to refer to a memorandum of the summer of 1942, I think, shortly after the decree of the Fuehrer came into force.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I have the following question to ask you: Is it possible that you . . .

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal will adjourn now. [The Tribunal recessed until 1400 hours.]

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Tell us, Defendant, who was the actual leader of the National Socialist Party in the Government General?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I hear nothing at all.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I ask you...

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I hear nothing at all.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I have the following question to put to you: After 6 May 1940 in the Government General...

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: 6 May?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, 6 May 1940, after the Nazi organization had been completed in the Government General, who was appointed its leader?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I was.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Thus the leadership of the administration of the National Socialist Party and of the Police was concentrated in your hands. Therefore you are responsible for the administration, the Police, and the political life of the Government General.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Before I answer that question, I must protest when you say that I had control of the Police.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I believe that that is the only way one could interpret the Fuehrer's orders and the other documents which I have put to you.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No doubt, if one disregards the actual facts and the realities of the situation.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Well, then, let us pass on to another group of questions. You heard of the existence of Maidanek only in 1944, isn't that so?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: In 1944 the name Maidanek was brought to my knowledge officially for the first time by the Press Chief Gassner.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I will now ask that you be shown a document which was presented by your defense counsel, which was compiled by you, and which is a report addressed to Hitler, dated June 1943. I will read into the record one excerpt, and I wish to remind you that this is dated 19 June 1943: "As a proof of the mistrust shown to the German leadership, I enclose a characteristic excerpt from the report of the Chief of the Security Police and SD in the Government General. . ."

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Just a moment. The wrong passage has been shown me. I have the passage here on Page 35 of the German text, and it is differently worded.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Have you found the place now?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes. But you started with a different sentence. The sentence here starts "A considerable part of the Polish intelligentsia . . ."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Which page is it?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Page 35 of the German text, last paragraph.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: It starts here with the words "A considerable part..."

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: All right. Then I will continue: "As a proof of the degree of the misfit shown to the German leadership I enclose"-these are your own words, this passage comes somewhat higher up in the quotation- "a characteristic excerpt from the report of the Chief of the Security Police and SD in the Government General for the period from 1 to 31 May 1943, concerning the possibilities of propaganda resulting from Katyn."

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That is not here. Would you be good enough to show me the passage? Now, what you are presenting here is not in my text.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, it is there; it comes somewhat earlier in your text.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I think it has been omitted from my text.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I begin now at that part which you find lower down at the bottom. Follow the text: "A large part of the Polish intelligentsia, however, as before, will not allow itself to be influenced by the news from Katyn and holds against the Germans alleged similar cruelties, especially in Auschwitz." I omit the next sentence and I continue: "Among that portion of the working classes which is not communistically inclined, this is scarcely denied; at the same time it is pointed out that the attitude of Germany towards the Poles is not any better." Please note the next sentence: "It is said that there are concentration camps at Auschwitz and Maidanek where likewise the mass murder of Poles is carried out systematically." How can one reconcile this part of your report which mentions Auschwitz and Maidanek, where mass murder took place, with your statement that you heard of Maidanek only at the end of 1944. Well, your report is dated June 1943; you mentioned there both Maidanek and Auschwitz.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: With reference to Maidanek we were talking about the extermination of Jews. The extermination of Jews in Maidanek became known to me during the summer of 1944. Up to now the word "Maidanek" has always been mentioned in connection with extermination of Jews.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, we are to understand-I refer to the text submitted to you-that in May 1943 you heard of the mass murder of Poles in Maidanek, and in 1944 you heard of the mass murder of Jews?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I beg your pardon? I heard about the extermination of the Jews at Maidanek in 1944 from the official documents in the foreign press.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And you heard of the mass killings of the Poles in 1943?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That is contained in my memorandum, and I protest; these are the facts as I put them before the Fuehrer.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I will ask that another document be shown to you. Do you know this document, are you acquainted with it?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: It is a decree dated 2 October 1943. I assume that the wording agrees with the text of the original decree.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, it is in full agreement with the original text. In any case your defense counsel can follow the text and will be able to verify it. I have to ask you one question. What do you think of this law signed by you?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes, it is here.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: You were President of the Reich Academy of Law. From the standpoint of the most elementary standards of law, what do you think of this law signed by you?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Have you got the number of it?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: It is Exhibit USSR-335, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: This is the general wording for a court-martial decree. It provides that the proceedings should take place in the presence of a judge, that a document should be drawn up, and that the proceedings should be recorded in writing. Apart from that I had the power to give pardons, so that every sentence had to be submitted to me.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I would like you to tell us how this court for court-martial proceedings was composed, who the members of this court were. Would you please pay attention to Paragraph 3, Point 1 of Paragraph 3?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: The Security Police, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: You were telling us of your hostile attitude to the SD. Why then did you give the SD the right to exert oppression on the Polish population?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Because that was the only way in which I could exert any influence on the sentences. If I had not published this decree, there would have been no possibility of control; and the Police would simply have acted at random.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: You spoke of the right of reprieve which was entrusted to you. Would you please note Paragraph 6 of this law. I remind you that a verdict of a summary court-martial by the SD was to be put into effect immediately according to the text. I remind you again that there was only one possible verdict: "death." How could you change it if the condemned person was to be shot or hanged immediately after the verdict?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: The sentence would nevertheless have to come before me.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, but a sentence had to be carried out immediately.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Those were the general instructions which I had issued in connection with the power given me to grant reprieves, and the committee which dealt with reprieves was constantly sitting. Files were sent in...

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Since you have spoken of the right to reprieve, I will put to you another question. Do you remember the AB Action?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Do you remember that this action signified the execution of thousands of Polish intellectuals?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Then what did it signify?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: It came within the framework of the general action of appeasement and it was my plan to eliminate, by means of a properly regulated procedure, arbitrary actions on the part of the Police This was the meaning of that action.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I do not understand very well what you mean. How did you treat persons who were subject to the AB Action? What happened to them?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: This meeting really only dealt with- the question of arrests.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I ask you what happened to them later?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: They were arrested and taken into protective custody.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And then?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Then they were subjected to the proceedings which had been established. At least, that is what I intended.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Was this left to the Police exclusively?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: The Police were in charge.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: In other words, the Police took over the extermination of these people after they had been arrested, is that so?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Well, then tell us, please, why you did not exercise your power of reprieve while they were carrying out this inhuman action?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I did make use of it.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I will put before you your statement, dated 30 May 1940. You certainly remember this meeting with the Police on 30 May 1940, when you gave final instructions to the police before carrying out this action?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: You stated the following: "Any attempt on the part of the legal authorities to intervene in the AB Action, undertaken with the help of the Police, should be considered as treason to the State and to German interests." Do you remember this statement?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I do not remember it, but you must take into account all the circumstances which spread over several weeks. You must consider the statement in its entirety and not seize upon one single sentence. This concerns a development which went on for weeks and months, in the course of which the reprieve committee was established by me for the first time. That was my way of protesting against arbitrary actions and of introducing legal justice in all these proceedings. That is a development extending over many weeks, which you cannot, in my opinion, summarize in one sentence.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am speaking of words which in my opinion can have only one meaning for a jurist. You wrote: "The reprieve committee which is part of my office is not concerned with these matters. The AB Action will be carried out exclusively by Higher SS and Police Leader Krueger and his organization. This is a purely internal action for quieting the country which is necessary and lies outside the scope of a normal legal trial." That is to say you renounced your right of pardon?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: At that particular moment; but if you follow the further development of the AB Action during the following weeks you will see that this never became effective. That was an intention, a bad intention, which, thank God, I gave up in time. Perhaps my defense counsel will be able to say a few words on the subject later.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: One single question interests me. Did you renounce your right of pardon while carrying out this operation or not?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Well then, how can you account for your words, this one sentence: "The reprieve committee is not concerned with these matters."? How should we interpret these words?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: This is not a decree; it is not the final ruling on the matter. It is a remark which was made on the spur of the moment and was then negotiated on for days. But one must recognize the final stage of the development, and not merely the various motives as they came up during the development.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, I understand that very well, Defendant. But I would like to ask you, was this statement made during a conference with the Police and did you instruct the Police in that matter?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Not during that meeting. I assume it came up in some other connection. Here we discussed only this one action. After all, I also had to talk to State Secretary Buehler.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Well, all right. While discussing the AB Action with the Police you stated that the results of this action would not concern the reprieve committee which was subordinated to you, is that right?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That sentence is contained in the diary. It is not, however, the final result, but rather an intermediate stage.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Perhaps I can recall to you another sentence, in order that you may judge the results of this action. Perhaps you can recall this part which I will put to you. You stated the following: "We need not bring these elements into German concentration camps, for in that case we would only have difficulties and an unnecessary correspondence with their families. We must simply liquidate matters in the country, and in the simplest way." What you mean is that this would simply be a question of liquidation in the simplest form, is that not so?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That is a terrible word. But, thank God, it did not take place in this way.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, but these persons were executed. What do you mean by saying that this was not carried out? Obviously this was carried out, for the persons were executed.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: When they were sentenced they were killed, if the right to pardon them was not exercised.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And they were condemned without application of the right of pardon?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I do not believe so.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Unfortunately these people are no more, and therefore obviously they; were executed.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Which people?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Those who were arrested under the AB Action. I will remind you of another excerpt connected with this AB Action. If you did not agree with the Police with regard to certain police actions it would be difficult to explain the celebrations in connection with the departure of Brigadefuehrer SS Streckenbach when he left for Berlin. Does this not mean that you were at least on friendly terms with the Police?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: In connection with political relations many words of praise are spoken which are not in keeping with the truth. You know that as well as any other person.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I will allow myself to remind you of only one passage of your speech addressed to the Brigadefuehrer Streckenbach, one sentence only. You said: "What you, Brigadefuehrer Streckenbach, and your people, have done in the Government General must not be forgotten; and you need not be ashamed of it." That testifies, does it not, to quite a different attitude toward Streckenbach and his people?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: And it was not forgotten either.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I have no further questions to put to the defendant.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does that conclude the cross-examination?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I have only one or two questions, if Your Honor pleases [Turning to the defendant.] In the course of your examination understood you to say that you had never gathered to yourself an, of the art treasures of the Government General. By that I do not suppose you to mean that you did not have them collected ant registered; you did have them collected and registered, isn't that so.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Art treasures in the Government General were official!: collected and registered. The book has been submitted here in Court

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Yes. And you told the Tribunal that before you go there one Duerer collection had already been seized-before you too} over your duties.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: May I ask you to understand that as follows: These were the Duerers which were removed in Lvov before the civilian administration was set up there. Herr Muehlmann went to Lvov at the time and took them from the library. I had never heel in Lvov before that. These pictures were then taken directly to the Fuehrer headquarters or to Reich Marshal Goering, I am not sun which.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: They were collected for Goering, that is what I an driving at. Is that not a fact?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: State Secretary Muehlmann, when I asked him, told me that he came on orders of the Reich Marshal and that he had taker them away on orders of the Reich Marshal

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: And were there not some other art objects that were collected by the Reich Marshal, and also by the Defendant Rosenberg, at the time you told the Tribunal you were too busy with tasks to get involved in that sort of thing?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I know of nothing of that sort in the Government General. The Einsatzstab Rosenberg had no jurisdiction in the Government General; and apart from the collection of the composer Elsner and a Jewish library from Lublin I had no official obligaton to demand the return of any art treasures from Rosenberg.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: But there were some art treasures in your possession when you were captured by the American forces.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes. They were not in my possession. I was safeguarding them but not for myself. They were also not in my immediate safekeeping; rather I had taken them along with me from burning Silesia. They could not be safeguarded any other way. They were art treasures which are so widely known that they are Numbers 1 to 10 in the list in the book-no one could have appropriated them. You cannot steal a "Mona Lisa."

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, I merely wanted to clear that up. I knew you had said on interrogation there were some in your possession. I am not trying to imply you were holding them for yourself, if you were not. However, I think you have made that clear.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I should like to remark in this connection, since I attach particular importance to the point, that these art treasures with which we are concerned could be safeguarded only in this way. Otherwise they would have been lost.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Very well. I have one other matter I would like to clear up and I will not be long. I understood you also to say this morning that you had struggled for some time to effect the release of the Krak6w professors who were seized and sent to Oranienburg soon after the occupation of Poland. Now, of course, you are probably familiar with what you said about it yourself in your diary, are you?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes, I said so this morning. Quite apart from what is said in the diary, what I said this morning is the truth. You must never forget that I had to speak among a circle of deadly enemies, people who reported every word I said to the Fuehrer and Himmler.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, of course, you recall that you suggested that they should have been retained in Poland, and liquidated or imprisoned there.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Never-not even if you confront me with this statement. I never did that. On the contrary, I received the professors from Krakow and talked to them quietly. Of all that happened I regretted that most of all.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Perhaps you do not understand me. I am talking about what you wrote in your own diary about these professors, and I shall be glad to read it to you and make it available to you if you care to contest it. You are not denying that you said they should either be returned for liquidation in Poland, or imprisoned in Poland, are you? You do not deny that?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I have just told you that I did say all that merely to hoodwink my enemies; in reality I liberated the professors. Nothing more happened to them after that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: All right. Were you also talking for special purposes when you gave General Krueger, the SS and Higher Police official, that fond farewell?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: The same applies also in this case. Permit me to say, sir, that I admit without reservation what can be admitted; but I have also sworn to add nothing. No one can admit any more than I have done by handing over these diaries. What I am asking is that you do not ask me to add anything to that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: No, I am not asking you to add anything to-it; rather, T was trying to clear it up, because you've made a rather difficult situation, perhaps, for yourself and for others. You see, if we cannot believe what you wrote in your diary, I don't know how you can ask us to believe what you say here. You were writing those things yourself, and at the time you wrote them I assume you didn't expect that you would be confronted with them.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does he not mean that this was a record of a speech that he has made?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: In his diary, yes. It is recorded in his diary.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: When he said, "I did that to hoodwink my enemies"?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I presume that that particular record is a record of some speech that he made.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: It is. It is entered in the diary.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: May I say something about that. It wasn't that I put myself in a difficult position; rather the changing course of the war made the situation difficult for every administrative official.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Finally, do you recall an entry in your diary in which you stated that you had a long hour and a half talk with the Fuehrer and that you had...

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: When was the last conference, please?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, this entry is on Monday, the 17th of March 1941. It's in your diary.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That was probably one of the very few conferences; whether I was alone with him, I don't know.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: . . . in which you said you and the Fuehrer had come to a complete agreement and that he approved all the measures, including all the decrees, especially also the entire organization of the country. Would you stand by that today?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No, but I might say the following: The Fuehrer's approval was always very spontaneously given, but one always had to wait a long while for it to be realized.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Was that one of the times you complained to him, as you told us this morning?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I constancy, complained. As you know, I offered TO resign on 14 occasions.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Yes, I know; but on this occasion did you make many complaints and did you have the approval of the Fuehrer, or did he turn down your complaints on this occasion of the 17th of March, 1941?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: The Fuehrer took a very simple way out at the time by saying, "You'll have to settle that with Himmler."

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, that isn't really an answer. You've entered in your diary that you talked it out with him and that he approved everything, and you make no mention in your diary of any disappointment over the filing of a complaint. Surely, this wasn't a speech that you were recording in your diary; it seems to be a factual entry on your conversations with the Fuehrer. And my question is simply, do you now admit that that was the situation, or are you saying that it was a false entry?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I beg your pardon, I didn't say that I made false entries. I never said that, and I'm not going to argue about words. I am merely saying that you must judge the words according to the entire context. If I emphasized in the presence of officials that the Fuehrer received me and agreed to my measures, then I did that to back up my own authority. I couldn't do that without the Fuehrer's agreement. What my thoughts were, is not made clear from this. I should like to emphasize that I'm not arguing about words and have not asked to do that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Very well, I don't care to press it any further.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Seidl, do you wish to re-examine?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, the first question put to you by the Soviet Prosecutor was whether you were the chief of the NSDAP in the Government General, and you answered "yes." Did the Party have any decisive influence in the Government General on political and administrative life?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No. The Party as an organization in that sphere was, of course, only nominally under my jurisdiction, for all the Party officials were appointed by Bormann without my being consulted. There is no special Fuehrer decree for the spheres of activity of the NSDAP in the occupied territories, in which it says that these spheres of activity are directly under Reichsleiter Bormann's jurisdiction.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Did your activity in that sphere of the NSDAP in the territory of the Government General have anything at all to do with any Security Police affairs?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: No, the Party was much too small to play any important part; it had no state function.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: The next question: The Soviet Prosecution showed you Document USSR-335. It is the Decree on Drumhead Courts-Martial of 1943. It states in Paragraph 6: "Drumhead court-martial sentences are to be carried out at once." Is it correct if I say that no formal legal appeal against these sentences was possible, but that a pardon was entirely admissible?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Certainly; but, nevertheless, I must say that this decree is impossible.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: What conditions in the Government General occasioned the issuing of this decree of 2 October 1943? I am thinking in particular of the security situation.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Looking back from the more peaceful conditions of the present time, I cannot think of any reason which might have made such a demand possible; but if one recalls the events of war, and the universal conflagration, it seems to have been a measure of desperation.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: I now come back to the AB Action. Is it true that in 1939 a court-martial decree was issued providing for considerably greater legal guarantees than that of 1943?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it correct that people arrested in the AB Action were, on the strength of this court-martial decree, sentenced or acquitted?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it also true that all sentences of these courts were, as you saw fit, to be passed on to the competent reprieve committee under State Secretary Buehler?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: The prosecutor of the United States has laid it to your charge that in Neuhaus, where you were arrested after the collapse of the German Armed Forces, various art treasures were found, not in your house, but in the office of the Governor General. Is it true that you sent State Secretary Dr. Buehler with a letter to Reich Minister Dr. Lammers, and that this letter contained a list of these art treasures?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Yes, not only that, I at once called the attention of the head of the Pinakothek in Munich to the fact that these pictures were there and that they should at once be safeguarded against bombing. He also looked at the pictures and then they were put in a bombproof cellar. I am glad I did so, for who knows what might otherwise have happened to these valuable objects.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: And now one last question. The Prosecution has submitted Document 661-PS. This document also has a USSR exhibit number, which I don't know at the moment. This is a document which has been made to have a bearing on the activities of the Academy for German Law, of which you were president. The document has the heading "Legal Formation of Germany's Polish Policy on Racial-Political Lines"; the legal part serves as a text for the Committee on the Law of Nationalities in the Academy for German Law. I'm having this document submitted to you. Please, will you tell me whether you've ever had this document in your hands before?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: From whom does it come?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: That is the extraordinary part; it has the Exhibit Number USA-300.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: Does it state anywhere who drew it up or something of the sort?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: The document has no author; nor does it show on whose order it was compiled.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: I can say merely that I've never seen the document; that I never gave an order for it to be drawn up; so I can say really nothing about it.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: It states here that it was found in the Ministry of Justice in Kassel. Was there a Ministry of Justice in Kassel in 1940?

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: A Ministry of Justice in Kassel?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FRANK
turns.text: That has not been in existence since 1866.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then the defendant can return to his seat.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: In that case, with the permission of the Tribunal, I shall call witness Dr.Bilfinger.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, Sir.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: This document which you produced as USSR-223, which are extracts from Defendant Frank's diary; are you offering that in evidence? Apparently some entries from Frank's diary have already been offered in evidence; others have not. Are you wishing to offer this in evidence?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: This document has already been submitted in evidence under two numbers; the first number is 2233-PS, which was submitted by the American Prosecution, and the second is Exhibit USSR-223, and was already submitted by us on 15 February, 1946.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I see. Have these entries which you have in this document been submitted under USSR-223? You see, the PS number does not necessarily mean that the documents have been offered in evidence. The PS numbers were applied to documents before they were offered in evidence; but the USSR-223 does imply that it has been offered in evidence.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: This document has already been presented in evidence.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov, what the Tribunal wants to know is whether you wish to offer this USSR-223 in evidence, because unless it was read before it hasn't been offered in evidence, or it hasn't gone into the record.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: We already read an excerpt on 15 February, and it is, therefore, already read into the record.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: well.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: May I retire, Mr. President?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes. [The witness Bilfinger took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you stand up, please, and will you tell us your full name?

turns.speaker: RUDOLF BILFINGER (Witness)
turns.text: Rudolf Bilfinger.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, since when were you active in the Reich Security Main Office (RSHA), and in what position?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: From the end of 1937 until the beginning of 1943 I was government councillor in the RSHA, and later senior government councillor and expert on legal questions, and legal questions in connection with the police.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it correct that on two occasions and at different times you were head of the "Administration and Law" department attached to the commander of the Security Police and SD in Krakow?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes. In the autumn of 1940 and in 1944 I was head of the department "Administration and Law" attached to the commander of the Security Police and SD in Krakow.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: What were the tasks you had to fulfil at different times in the Government General-in broad outline.

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: In 1940 I had the task of taking over from the Government General a number of branches of the police administration and working in that connection under the Higher SS and Police Leader.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: What was the legal position of the Higher SS and Police Leader, and what was his relation to the Governor General? Did the Higher SS and Police Leader receive his instructions concerning the Security Police and the SD from the Governor General? Or did he receive them direct from the Reichsfuehrer SS and Chief of the Police, that is, Himmler?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: The Higher SS and Police Leader from the very beginning received his instructions direct from the Reichsfuehrer SS, Himmler.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Is it furthermore true that the commander of the Security Police and of the SD in the Government General also received direct orders and instructions from Amt IV, the Gestapo, and from Amt V, the Criminal Police in the RSHA?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes, the commander of the Security Police received many orders direct from the various departments of the RSHA, particularly from departments IV and V.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Did the institution of the State Secretariat for Security, which. occurred in 1942, bring about a change in the legal position of the Governor General with reference to measures of the Security Police and the SD?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: The appointment of a State Secretary as such did not alter the legal position of the Governor General or of the State Secretary. New spheres of activity were merely added to the State Secretariat for Security.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Do you know of a decree of Reichsfuehrer SS and Chief of the German Police, Himmler, in the year 1939, and what were its contents?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I knew of a decree, probably dated 1939, dealing with the appointment of the Higher SS and Police Leader, which ruled that the Higher SS and Police Leader would receive his instructions direct from Himmler. DR" SEIDL: The institution of the State Secretariat dated from 7 May 1942 and was based on a Fuehrer decree. The application of this decree called forth another decree dated 3 June 1942, which dealt with the transfer of official business to the State Secretary for Security. Do you know the contents of that decree?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: The essential contents of the decrees which you have mentioned are known to me.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it correct that on the basis of this decree the entire Political Police and the Criminal Police, as had been the case before, were again subordinated to the State Secretary for Security within the framework of the Security Police?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: These two branches from the very beginning were under the Higher SS and Police Leader, and later on under the State Secretary for Security. To this extent the decree did not bring about a change, but was merely a confirmation.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Is it known to you that in Appendix B of that decree there are 26 paragraphs in which all the branches of the Security Police are transferred to the Higher SS and Police Chief as State Secretary for Security?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Do you know that in this decree, in Appendix B. Jewish matters are also mentioned specifically?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Do you know that in Paragraph 21 of Appendix B it is ruled: "The special fields of the Security Police: Representation of the Government General at conferences and meetings, particularly with the central offices of the Reich, which deal with the above-mentioned special fields."?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I know that as far as the sense is concerned, such a ruling was contained therein. Whether Paragraph 21 or another paragraph was worded this way I don't remember.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it also true that on the basis of this decree the last remains of the administrative police were removed from the administration of the Government General and handed over to the State Secretary for Security, who was directly under Himmler.

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: That was the intention and the purpose of this decree. But, contrary to the wording of that decree, only a few branches were taken away from the administration; concerning the remainder a fight ensued later. The result was, however, that all branches of the police administration were taken away.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, did the administration of the Government General have anything to do with the establishment and administration of concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: To the best of my knowledge, no.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: You were with the Chief of the Security Police and SD in Krakow. When did you yourself hear of concentration camps at Maidanek, Treblinka, and Lublin for the first time?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: May I correct you, I was attached to the Commander of Security Police.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Yes, the Commander of the Security Police.

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I heard of Maidanek for the first time when Lublin and Maidanek were occupied by the Russians; and through propaganda I heard for the first time what the name Maidanek meant, when the then Governor General Frank ordered an investigation regarding events in Maidanek and responsibility for these events.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: According to your own observation, generally speaking, what were the relations like between the Govern or General and the SS Obergruppenfuehrer Krueger, and what were the reasons for those relations?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Relations between them were very bad from the beginning. The reasons were partly questions of organization and of the use of the Police, and partly essential differences of opinion.

turns.speaker: DR SEIDL
turns.text: What do you mean by essential differences of opinion? Do you mean different opinions regarding the treatment of the Polish population?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I can still recollect one example which concerned the confirmation of police court-martial sentences by Governor General Frank. I opposition to Krueger's opinion, he either failed to confirm a number of sentences or else mitigated them considerably. In this connection I remember such differences of opinion.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Were these sentences which were passed in connection with the so-called AB Action?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I know nothing of an AB Action.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: You came to the Government General later, did you?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I came to the Government General in August 1940.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: I have no further questions for this witness. Are PRESIDENT: Do any of the defendants' counsel want to ask questions?

turns.speaker: DR. RUDOLF MERKEL (Counsel for Gestapo)
turns.text: May I put a few questions to the witness? Witness, the Prosecution states that the State Police was a circle of persons formed in accordance with a common plan, and that membership in it was voluntary. Since you had an especially high position in the RSHA, I ask you to tell me briefly what you know about these questions?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Of the members of the Secret State Police only a small part were volunteers. The former officials, the officials of the former political department of the headquarters of the Commissioner of the Police, constituted the nucleus of the membership of the Secret State Police. The various local police head of flees were created from these former political departments of the central police headquarters, and at the same time practically all the officials from these former political departments were taken over. In Berlin, for example, it was Department I-A of the central police headquarters. Apart from that, administrative officials were transferred from other administrative authorities to the Secret State Police, or were detailed to go here. As time went on people from administrations and offices were forced to transfer to the Secret State Police. Thus, for instance, the entire frontier customs service was transferred to the Secret State Police in 1944 by order of the Fuehrer. At about the same time the whole of the intelligence service was transferred. In the course of the war numerous members of the Waffen-SS who were no longer eligible for active military service were detailed to the Secret State Police. In addition many people who originally had had nothing to do with police work were drafted as emergency members to the Secret State Police.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: If I summarize it by saying that the Secret State Police was a Reich authority and that the German civil service law applied to its employees, is that correct?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Was it possible for the officials to resign from the Secret State Police easily?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: It was extremely difficult and, in fact, impossible to resign from the Secret State Police. One could resign only in very special circumstances.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: It has been stated here with reference to the composition of the Secret State Police personnel that there was the following proportion: executive officers about 20 percent; administrative officials about 20 percent; and technical personnel approximately 60 percent. Are these figures about right?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I have no general information about the composition of the personnel; but for certain offices about which I knew more these figures would probably apply.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Under whose jurisdiction were the concentration camps in Germany and in the occupied countries?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: The concentration camps were under the jurisdiction of the Economic Administration Main office under SS Gruppenfuehrer Pohl.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Did the Secret State Police have anything to do with the administration of the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: No. It may be that at the beginning certain concentration camps here and there were administered directly by the Secret State Police for a short period. That was probably the case in individual instances. But in principle even at that time, and later on without exception, the concentration camps were administered by the Economic Administration Main Office.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know at all who gave orders for the liquidations which took place in the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: No, I know nothing about that.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Can you say anything about the grounds for protective custody? On the strength of what legal rulings was protective custody decreed after 1933?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Protective custody was based on the Decree of the Reich President for the Protection of the People and the State, of February 1933, in which a number of the basic rights of the Weimar Constitution were rescinded.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Was there later a decree by the Minister of the Interior which dealt with protective custody, at the end of 1936 or the beginning of 1937?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes, at that time the Protective Custody Law was drawn up. The legal basis as such remained in force. At that time power to decree protective custody was confined to the Secret State Police. Before that a number of other offices, rightly or wrongly, had decreed protective custody. To prevent this, protective custody was then confined to the Secret State Police.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Is it correct that for some time you were in France. In what capacity were you there?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: In the late summer and autumn of 1943 I was commander of the Security Police in France, in Toulouse.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know anything about an order from the RSHA, or from the commander of the Sipo for France, or from individual district commanders, to the effect that ill-treatment or torture was to be applied when prisoners were interrogated?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: No, I do not know of such orders.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Then how do you explain the ill-treatment and atrocities which actually took place in connection with interrogations, proof of which has been given by the Prosecution?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: It is possible that ill-treatment did occur; in a number of cases this either took place in spite of its being forbidden, or else it was committed by members of other German offices in France which did not belong to the Security Police.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Did you, while you were active in France, hear of any such ill-treatment either officially or by hearsay?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I never heard of any such ill-treatment at the hands of members of the German police or the German Armed Forces. I heard only of cases of ill-treatment carried out by groups consisting of Frenchmen who were being employed by some German authority.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Were there so-called Gestapo prisons in France?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: No, the Security Police in France did not have prisons of their own. They handed over their prisoners to the detention camps of the German Armed Forces.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: One last question: The Prosecution has given proof of a large number of crimes against humanity and war crimes which were committed with the participation of the Security Police. Can one say that these crimes were perfectly obvious and were known to all members of the Secret State Police, or were these crimes known only to a small circle of persons who had been ordered directly to carry out the measures concerned? Do you know anything about that?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: I didn't quite understand the question from the beginning. Were you referring to France or to the Security Police in general?

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: I was referring to the Security Police in general.

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: No ill-treatment or torture of any kind was permitted; and, as far as I know, nothing of the kind did happen, still less was it known generally or to a larger circle of persons. I knew nothing about it:

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We will adjourn now for 10 minutes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does the Prosecution wish to cross-examine? Is there nothing you wish to ask arising from Dr. Merkel's cross-examination, Dr. Seidl?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: I have only one more question to ask the witness. Witness, in Paragraph 4 of the decree of 23 June 1942 the following ruling is made, and I quote: "The SS and Police Leaders in the districts are directly subordinate to the governors of the districts, Just as the State Secretary for Security is subordinate to the Governor General." Thus it does not say that the entire police organization is subordinate, but only the police leaders. Now I ask you whether orders which had been issued by the commanders of the Security Police and the SD were forwarded to the governors or were sent directly to the district chiefs of the Security Police and the SD?

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: These orders were always sent directly from the commander to the district chiefs d the Security Police and the SD. The commander could give no instructions to the governors.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: If I understand you correctly you mean that the Security Police and the SD had their own official channels which had absolutely nothing to do with the administrative construction of the Government General.

turns.speaker: BILFINGER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: I have no further questions for the witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: With the permission of the Tribunal, I call as the next witness the former Governor of Krakow, Dr. Kurt von Burgsdorff. [The witness Von Burgsdorff took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you state your full name?

turns.speaker: KURT VON BURGSDORFF (Witness)
turns.text: Kurt von Burgsdorff

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: "I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing." [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, the Government General was divided into five districts at the head of each of which there was a governor; is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: From 1 December 1943 until the occupation of your district by Soviet troops you were governor of the district Krakow?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes. To use the correct official term, I was . . .

turns.speaker: GENERAL R. A. RUDENKO (Chief Prosecutor for the U.S.S.R.)
turns.text: Mr. President, the defense counsel has put the question of the "occupation" of this region by Soviet troops. I energetically protest against such terminology and consider it a hostile move.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Mr. President, I have just been told that perhaps a mistake in the translation has crept in. All I intended to say was that, in the course of the year 1944, the area of which this witness was governor was occupied by the Soviet troops in the course of military action. I do not know what the Soviet prosecutor is protesting against; it is at any rate far from my intention to make any hostile statement here.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I think the point was, it was not an occupation, it was a liberation by the Russian Army.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Of course; I did not want to say any more than that the German troops were driven out of this area by the Soviet troops. Witness, will you please continue with your answer?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I was entrusted with exercising the duties of a governor-that is the correct official expression. Until a few months ago I was still an officer of the Wehrmacht, and during my entire activity in Krakow I remained an officer of the Wehrmacht.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, according to your observations, what basically was the attitude of the Governor General toward the Polish and Ukrainian people?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I want to emphasize that I can answer only for the year 1944. At that time the attitude of the Governor General was that he wished to live in peace with the people.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it correct that already in 1942 the Governor General had given the governors the opportunity of setting up administrative committees, comprised of Poles and Ukrainians, attached to the district chiefs?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: There was a governmental decree to this effect. Whether that was in 1942 or not I do not know.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Did you yourself make use of the authorization contained therein, and did you establish such administrative committees?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: In the district of Krakow I had such a committee established at once for every district chief.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, according to your observations what was the food situation like in the Government General, and particularly in your district?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: It was not unsatisfactory; but I must add that the reason for that was that, in addition to the rations, the Polish population had an extensive black market.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: According to your observations what was the attitude of the Governor General on the question of the mobilization of labor?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: He did not wish any workers sent outside the Government General, because he was interested in retaining the necessary manpower within the country.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Was the Church persecuted by the Governor General in the Government General; and what basically was the attitude of the Governor General to this question, according to your observations?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Again I can answer only for my district and for the year 1944. There was no persecution of the Church; on the contrary, the relations with churches of all denominations were good in my district. On my travels I always received the clergy, and I never heard any complaint.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Did you have any personal experience with the Governor General with regard to this question?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes. In the middle of January 1944 I was appointed District Standortfuehrer by the Governor General, who at the same time was the Party Leader in the Government General; that is, I was appointed to a Party, office for the district of Krakow. I pointed out to him, as I had pointed out to the Minister of the Interior, Himmler, before, that I was a convinced church-going Christian. The Governor General replied that he was in no way perturbed by that and that he knew of no provision in the Party program which prohibited it.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: What, according to your observations, were the relations like between the Governor General and the administration of the Government General on the one side, and the Security Police and the SD on the other side?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Doubtlessly underneath they were bad, because the Police always ended by doing only what it wanted and did not concern itself with the administration. For that reason in he country districts also there was real friction between the administration offices and the Police.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Is it correct that when you took office, or shortly after, the Governor General issued several instructions referring to the Police? I quote from the diary of the Defendant Dr. Frank, the entry of 4 January 1944: "The Governor General then gave some instructions to Dr. Von Burgsdorff with reference to his new activities. His task will be to inform himself, as a matter of principle, of all decisive factors in the district. Above all the Governor should direct his efforts to opposing energetically any encroachments by the Police."

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Today I no longer remember that conversation of 4 January 1944, but it may have taken place. However, I do remember that after I took office, at the end of November 1943, I went to see the Governor General once more and told him that I had heard that the relations with the Police were not good and were scarcely tolerable for the administration. He replied that he was doing what he could in order, as I might put it, to bring the Police to reason. It was on the basis of this statement by the Governor General that I definitely decided to remain in the Government General. I had, as is known, told the Reich Minister of the Interior that I was unwilling to go there.

turns.speaker: DR SEIDL
turns.text: In your capacity as Governor did you have any authority to issue commands to the Security Police and the SD in your district?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: None whatsoever.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Did you yourself ever see a police directive?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Never. With the Police, orders are passed down vertically, that is, directly from the Higher SS and Police Leader to the SS and Police Leader respectively-and that is probably the usual way-from the chief of the Security Police to the unit commander of the Security Police.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: In your activity as Governor did you have anything to do with the administration of concentration camps?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Never.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Do you know who administered the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No, not from my own experience; but I have heard that there was some central office in Berlin under the Reichsfuehrer SS.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: When did you hear for the first time of the Maidanek concentration camp?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: From you, about a fortnight ago.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: You want to tell the Tribunal under oath . . .

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: . . . that you, although you were Governor of Krakow In the occupied Polish territory, did not learn about that until during your captivity?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes, I am firmly convinced that I heard about this concentration camp from you for the first time.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: When did you for the first time hear of the Treblinka concentration camp?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Also from you on the same occasion.

turns.speaker: DR.SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, the Governor General is accused by the Prosecution of issuing a summary court-martial decree in the year 1943. What at that time was the security situation in the Government General?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Again I can judge only for the year 1944. As the German troops came back from the East, it became worse and worse, so that in my district it became increasingly difficult to carry out any kind of administration.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: According to your observations what was the economic situation like in the agricultural and industrial sectors of your district, and is the statement justified that, allowing for wartime conditions, the administration of the Government General had done everything to promote economy?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Economy in my district was at full force in 1944 both in industry and in agriculture. Some industries had been transferred from the Reich to the Government General; and, as far as agriculture was concerned, the administration imported large quantities of fertilizers and seeds and the like. Horse breeding was also greatly promoted in my district.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: The Defendant Dr. Frank is accused of not having done everything that was necessary with regard to public health and sanitary conditions. What can you say about this point?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I can say that in my district-again speaking of 1944-hospitals were improved and new ones installed. A great deal was done, especially in the. fighting of epidemics. Typhus, dysentery, and typhoid were greatly reduced by inoculation.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: The Defendant Frank is also accused of having neglected higher education. Do you know anything about the conditions in the Government General in regard to this?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: When I came into the Government General there was no longer any higher education at all. On the basis of other experiences I suggested immediately that Polish universities be opened again. I contacted the president of the main department for education, who told me that the government was already entertaining such plans. In every one of my monthly reports I pointed cut the necessity for Polish universities, because within a short time, or more correctly in a few years' time, there would be a shortage of technicians, doctors, and veterinarians.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Now, one last question. There was a so-called sphere of activity of the NSDAP in the Government General; you were the District Standortfuehrer in the Government General?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: Witness, what, according to your observations, were the relations between the Governor General and the Head of the Party Chancellery, Bormann?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I believe I can say without exaggeration that they were extremely bad. As District Standortfuehrer I combined this office with that of District Governor and witnessed the last great struggle of the Governor General against Bormann. The Governor General held the view, and in this he was justified, that it was wrong to combine the Party office with the government office. He was afraid there would be too much interference not only by the Police but also by the Party, and he wanted to prevent that. Bormann, on the other hand, wanted to establish the predominance of the Party over the State in the Government General as well. That led to the most serious conflict.

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: I have no further questions for the witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any of the other Defense Counsel wish to ask any other questions?

turns.speaker: DR. OTTO FREIHERR VON LUEDINGHAUSEN (Counsel for Defendant Von Neurath)
turns.text: Witness, you were at one time Under State Secretary in the Government of the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia? When was that?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: From the end of March 1939 until the middle of March 1942.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: And to whom were you directly subordinate as Under State Secretary? The State Secretary Frank or the Reich Protector?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: State Secretary Frank.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: How did you come to know about the activities of Von Neurath as Reich Protector?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: From conferences with him and personal conversations.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: What kind of work did you have to do as Under State Secretary?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I was in charge of the administration proper.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Were the Police and the various SS and police offices subordinate to you?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: To whom were they subordinate?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: To State Secretary Frank.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: What was State Secretary Frank's attitude to Von Neurath?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: You mean officially?

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Officially, yes, of course.

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Herr Von Neurath tried at first to get on with Herr Frank; but the stronger Frank's position became, the more impossible that was. State Secretary Frank, later Minister Frank, had behind him the entire power of the SS and the Police, and finally Hitler also.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: From whom did Frank get his orders directly?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: As far as I know, from Himmler; however, I saw that on one or two or three occasions he received direct orders from Hitler.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: And that happened mostly without Von Neurath being consulted?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: That I cannot say, but I assume so.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Was it possible for Frank to perform his political functions independently within his sphere of activity, or did he have to have the approval of Herr Von Neurath?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Whether he was authorized or allowed to do so, I should not like to decide, but at any rate he did so.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Were Herr Von Neurath and Herr Frank of the same opinion concerning the policy towards the Czech people?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I did not understand your question.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Did Herr Von Neurath agree with the policy toward the Czech people pursued by Frank or his superior, Himmler?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Could he carry through his aims?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: He could not do anything, confronted as he was by Himmler's and Hitler's immense power.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: What was Herr Von Neurath's own policy and attitude?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: At the beginning I spoke very often about these things to Herr Von Neurath. On the basis of the decree of 15 March he hoped and believed he could get the Germans and Czechs in the Protectorate to live together reasonably and peacefully.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: But as Frank's position became stronger, that became more and more difficult?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you remember that in the middle of November 1939 serious disturbances broke out among the students in Prague?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you also remember that on the day after these incidents Herr Von Neurath and Frank flew to Berlin?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you remember that Frank returned from Berlin alone on the same day?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I believe I can recall that Frank returned on the same day, but I do not know whether he returned alone.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: You don't know whether Herr Von Neurath returned with him?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know anything else about the incidents connected with the students' disturbances and what the consequences were?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: They resulted, as far as I remember, in the execution of several students and in the closing of the universities.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know whether the universities were closed on Himmler's order?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know anything about the attitude of Herr Von Neurath towards the Catholic and Protestant Churches?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: His attitude was always above reproach, and there were no difficulties with the churches during the time that I was in the Protectorate.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know that Herr Von Neurath was in contact with the Archbishop of Prague until the latter's death?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No, I don't know anything about that.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know anything concerning whether, during the term of office of Herr Von Neurath, with his approval or upon his orders, art treasures of any kind, pictures, monuments, sculptures, libraries, or the like, belonging either to the State or to private owners, were confiscated and removed from the country?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: It is certain, absolutely certain, that he did not order anything of the sort. Whether he consented in any way to this I do not know, but I do not believe so. I remember one incident in the Malta Palace, where some Reich office-I don't remember today which it was-removed art treasures. Herr Von Neurath immediately did everything to make good this damage.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know that the customs union which had been ordered by Berlin from the very beginning between the Protectorate and Germany was not established for a long time because of Herr Von Neurath's intervention?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes. I definitely know about that. However, in the interest of the truth, I have to add that State Secretary Frank also was against the customs union, because, like Herr Von Neurath, he believed that the economy of the Protectorate would be damaged by the stronger economy of Germany.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: While Von Neurath was Reich Protector, was there any compulsory deportation of workers?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I am convinced that that did not happen. Workers were recruited, but in an entirely regular manner. That was the case while I was in the Protectorate.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know whether Von Neurath made travel in or out of the Protectorate dependent on official approval?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Whether or not Von Neurath did that, I do not know.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know anything about the closing of the secondary schools?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: What do you know about it?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I remember that the closing of the secondary schools was a necessary consequence of the closing of the universities. There were too many secondary schools in the Protectorate. Not all of them were closed by any means. On the other hand technical schools were greatly expanded and new ones established. I cannot remember anything more exact about it.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: Do you know anything about Von Neurath's attitude towards the Germanization of Czechoslovakia as intended by Himmler?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes, I remember the memorandum which Von Neurath sent to Hitler about the whole affair. That memorandum was intended to defer Himmler's plans for forced Germanization. Von Neurath expressed the view, which he had frequently mentioned to me, that in the interest of peace in the Protectorate he did not advocate these attempts at Germanization.

turns.speaker: DR. VON LUEDINGHAUSEN
turns.text: I have no more questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does the Prosecution wish to cross-examine?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Tell us, please, when you first joined the National Socialist Party?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: On 1 May 1933.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: And did you achieve office in any of its affiliated organizations?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I was an honorary SA Gruppenfuehrer.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Any other honors?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Then for a few years, just as I had been during the democratic regime, I was legal advisor to the administration of Saxony.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Weren't you also an Oberbannfuehrer in the HJ, the Hitler-Jugend ?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I once became Oberbannfuehrer on the occasion of the Reich Youth Leader's visit to Prague. But that was purely a gesture of courtesy, which had no consequences. I should like to mention again, since you speak of Party offices, that, as was said before, because of my post as Governor of Krak6w I was District Standortfuehrer from the middle of January 1944 until the end, that is the middle of January 1945.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You also received the gold badge of the Hitler Youth, did you not?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Weren't you in some way associated with Reinhard Heydrich when you were in Prague?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: I was with Heydrich until the middle of 1942. Then, as is generally known, because of the course pursued by Heydrich, I left the Protectorate, and at 55 years of age I went into the army.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: What position did you occupy with relation to Heydrich?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: The same as under Herr Von Neurath; I was Under State Secretary.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Let me put it to you this way: You told us that you never heard of Maidanek, the concentration camp?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: And you never heard of Auschwitz?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Of Auschwitz, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Had you heard of an installation known as Lublin?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Of Lublin? Not of the concentration camp but of the city of Lublin, of course.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you know of a concentration camp by the name of Lublin?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You did know I assume, of many other concentration camps by name?

turns.speaker: VON BURGSDORFF
turns.text: Only of German camps, yes-of Dachau and Buchenwald.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: That is ale

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Have you any questions?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: I have no more questions for the witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Who is your next witness?

turns.speaker: DR. SEIDL
turns.text: The next witness would be the former secretary of the Governor General, Fraulein Kraffczyk. However, if I understood the Tribunal correctly yesterday, this session will end at 1630 hours.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal will adjourn now until Tuesday morning. [The Tribunal adjourned until 23 April 1946 at 1000 hours.]
